metadata.document_title: Drug-Induced Liver Injury: Premarketing Clinical Evaluation: Guidance for Industry

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must evaluate premarketing clinical trial data for signals of drug-induced liver injury (DILI), with the goal of distinguishing drugs likely to cause severe liver injury (irreversible liver failure that is fatal or requires liver transplantation) from those that do not.
rules.source_section: I. Introduction
rules.verbatim_quote: This guidance is intended to assist the pharmaceutical industry and other investigators who are conducting new drug development in assessing the potential for a drug to cause severe liver injury (i.e., irreversible liver failure that is fatal or requires liver transplantation).

rules.rule_text: DILI has been the most frequent single cause of safety-related drug marketing withdrawals. Sponsors must evaluate hepatotoxic potential during premarketing development to prevent approval of drugs with unacceptable severe DILI risk.
rules.source_section: II. Background: DILI
rules.verbatim_quote: DILI has been the most frequent single cause of safety-related drug marketing withdrawals for the past 50 years.

rules.rule_text: Hy's Law is the most specific predictor of severe DILI. A Hy's Law case consists of three components: (1) the drug causes hepatocellular injury shown by a higher incidence of ≥3-fold ALT or AST elevation above ULN versus control; (2) among subjects with AT elevations, one or more also show TBL >2xULN without initial findings of cholestasis (elevated ALP); (3) no other reason can explain the combination of increased AT and TBL.
rules.source_section: III. Signals of DILI and Hy's Law
rules.verbatim_quote: Briefly, Hy's Law cases have the following three components: 1. The drug causes hepatocellular injury, generally shown by a higher incidence of 3-fold or greater elevations above the ULN of ALT or AST than the (nonhepatotoxic) control drug or placebo 2. Among trial subjects showing such AT elevations, often with ATs much greater than 3xULN, one or more also show elevation of serum TBL to >2xULN, without initial findings of cholestasis (elevated serum ALP) 3. No other reason can be found to explain the combination of increased AT and TBL, such as viral hepatitis A, B, or C; preexisting or acute liver disease; or another drug capable of causing the observed injury
exceptions: Patients with preexisting liver disease (NASH, chronic hepatitis C or B)
Patients on drugs that inhibit bilirubin glucuronidation (indinavir or atazanavir)

rules.rule_text: Finding one Hy's Law case in a clinical trial database is worrisome; finding two is considered highly predictive that the drug has the potential to cause severe DILI when given to a larger population. The finding of two Hy's Law cases, and probably even one, is a strong predictor of a significant risk of severe liver injury.
rules.source_section: III. Signals of DILI and Hy's Law
rules.verbatim_quote: Finding one Hy's Law case in the clinical trial database is worrisome; finding two is considered highly predictive that the drug has the potential to cause severe DILI when given to a larger population... the finding of two Hy's Law cases, and probably even one, is a strong predictor of a significant risk of severe liver injury.

rules.rule_text: Severe DILI can be estimated to occur at a rate of at least one-tenth the rate of Hy's Law cases. Thus, if no Hy's Law cases (AT and bilirubin elevations) are seen in 3,000 well-observed subjects, it can be concluded with 95% confidence that the true rate is not more than 1 per 1,000 (Rule of 3 from binomial calculation).
rules.source_section: III. Signals of DILI and Hy's Law
rules.verbatim_quote: Severe DILI can be estimated to occur at a rate of at least one-tenth the rate of the so-called Hy's Law cases (Temple 2001)... if no cases of AT and bilirubin elevations are seen in 3,000 well-observed subjects, it can be concluded with 95 percent confidence that the true rate of such occurrences is not more than 1 per 1,000.

rules.rule_text: An excess of AT elevations to >3xULN compared to a control group is a sensitive but non-specific signal for severe DILI potential. This signal has high sensitivity but low specificity because drugs that do not cause severe DILI (e.g., tacrine, statins, aspirin, heparin) can also produce this finding.
rules.source_section: III. Signals of DILI and Hy's Law — AT Elevations >3xULN
rules.verbatim_quote: An excess of AT elevations to >3xULN compared to a control group... the sensitivity of a significantly increased incidence compared to control (e.g., of >3xULN AT elevations) as an indicator of a potential for liver injury is high. But many drugs show this signal without conferring a risk of severe injury (e.g., tacrine, statins, aspirin, heparin), indicating low specificity for an excess of AT elevations alone.

rules.rule_text: One or more cases of newly elevated total serum bilirubin to >2xULN in a setting of pure hepatocellular injury, with no other explanation and accompanied by an overall increased incidence of AT elevations >3xULN in the test drug group compared to placebo, constitutes the most specific signal (Hy's Law case) indicating severe DILI potential.
rules.source_section: III. Signals of DILI and Hy's Law — Hy's Law Signal Definition
rules.verbatim_quote: One or more cases of newly elevated total serum bilirubin to >2xULN in a setting of pure hepatocellular injury (no evidence of obstruction, such as elevated ALP typical of gall bladder or bile duct disease, or malignancy, or impaired glucuronidation capacity caused by genetic (Gilbert syndrome) or pharmacologic (treatment with atazanavir or other drugs) factors), with no other explanation (viral hepatitis, alcoholic or autoimmune hepatitis, other hepatotoxic drugs), accompanied by an overall increased incidence of AT elevations >3xULN in the test drug group compared to placebo
exceptions: Atazanavir or other drugs inhibiting bilirubin metabolism

rules.rule_text: Patients with stable underlying liver disease generally should be included in at least some phase 3 trials if they are likely to be treated with the drug if it is marketed. Patients with acute viral, autoimmune, alcoholic, or other hepatitis types that are unstable are generally not appropriate subjects for clinical trials other than trials of treatments for their acute illness.
rules.source_section: IV.A.1. Patients with Liver Abnormalities or Disease
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients with stable underlying liver disease generally should be included in at least some phase 3 trials if they are likely to be treated with the drug if it is marketed. Patients with acute viral, autoimmune, alcoholic, or other types of hepatitis are unstable and generally not appropriate subjects for clinical trials other than trials of treatments for their acute illness.
exceptions: Patients with impaired bilirubin excretory or protein synthetic functions should generally not be enrolled unless there is strong need

rules.rule_text: Diagnostic screening for liver test abnormalities must be conducted before enrolling subjects into clinical trials to establish baseline values and to avoid confounding DILI signal detection.
rules.source_section: IV.A.1. Patients with Liver Abnormalities or Disease
rules.verbatim_quote: This implies that diagnostic screening for liver test abnormalities should be conducted before enrolling subjects into trials.

rules.rule_text: Early clinical trials in subjects with presumably normal liver function must obtain liver enzyme (ALT, AST, ALP) and bilirubin tests every 2 to 4 weeks, at least for a few months. For drugs with short treatment courses, both baseline and post-treatment liver enzyme testing must be performed because there may be a gap between end of treatment and onset of liver injury.
rules.source_section: IV.A.2. Detection of DILI
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, early trials of a drug in trial subjects with presumably normal liver function should involve obtaining liver enzyme (ALT, AST, ALP) and bilirubin tests every 2 to 4 weeks, at least for a few months. For drugs being studied with short treatment courses, both baseline and post-treatment liver enzyme testing should be performed, since there may be a gap between the end of treatment and the onset of liver injury.
exceptions: In longer trials, if no sign of liver injury after 3 months, monitoring interval can increase to every 2-3 months

rules.rule_text: If symptoms compatible with DILI precede knowledge of serum chemical test abnormalities, liver enzyme measurements must be made immediately, regardless of when the next visit or monitoring interval is scheduled. Attention to symptoms does not supplant routine periodic assessment of AT, TBL, and ALP.
rules.source_section: IV.A.2. Detection of DILI
rules.verbatim_quote: If symptoms compatible with DILI precede knowledge of serum chemical test abnormalities, liver enzyme measurements should be made immediately, regardless of when the next visit or monitoring interval is scheduled... Attention to symptoms does not supplant routine periodic assessment of AT, TBL, and ALP in trials of investigational drugs.

rules.rule_text: An increase of serum AT to >3xULN must be followed by repeat testing within 48 to 72 hours of all four serum measures (ALT, AST, ALP, and TBL) to confirm abnormalities and determine if they are increasing or decreasing. Delay in retesting may allow progression to severe worsening.
rules.source_section: IV.A.3. Confirmation
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, an increase of serum AT to >3xULN should be followed by repeat testing within 48 to 72 hours of all four of the usual serum measures (ALT, AST, ALP, and TBL) to confirm the abnormalities and to determine if they are increasing or decreasing.
exceptions: For outpatient trials where subjects cannot return promptly, subjects should be retested locally with results made available to investigators immediately

rules.rule_text: Close observation must be initiated immediately upon detection and confirmation of early signals of possible DILI. Close observation must not wait until the next scheduled visit or monitoring interval. Close observation includes: repeating liver enzyme and bilirubin tests 2-3 times weekly; ruling out alternative causes (viral hepatitis A, B, C, D, E; NASH; autoimmune hepatitis; biliary tract disease; cardiovascular causes); obtaining additional liver function tests (INR, direct bilirubin); and considering hepatology consultation.
rules.source_section: IV.A.4. Close Observation
rules.verbatim_quote: It is critical to initiate close observation immediately upon detection and confirmation of early signals of possible DILI, and not to wait until the next scheduled visit or monitoring interval... Close observation includes: Repeating liver enzyme and serum bilirubin tests two or three times weekly... Ruling out acute viral hepatitis types A, B, C, D, and E; autoimmune or alcoholic hepatitis; NASH; hypoxic/ischemic hepatopathy; and biliary tract disease.

rules.rule_text: Discontinuation of trial drug treatment must be considered when any of the following criteria are met: (1) ALT or AST >8xULN; (2) ALT or AST >5xULN for more than 2 weeks; (3) ALT or AST >3xULN AND (TBL >2xULN OR INR >1.5); (4) ALT or AST >3xULN with symptoms of fatigue, nausea, vomiting, right upper quadrant pain or tenderness, fever, rash, and/or eosinophilia (>5%).
rules.source_section: IV.A.5. Decision to Stop Drug Administration
rules.verbatim_quote: Discontinuation of treatment should be considered if: ALT or AST >8xULN; ALT or AST >5xULN for more than 2 weeks; ALT or AST >3xULN and (TBL >2xULN or INR >1.5); ALT or AST >3xULN with the appearance of fatigue, nausea, vomiting, right upper quadrant pain or tenderness, fever, rash, and/or eosinophilia (>5%)
exceptions: Automatic discontinuation upon >3xULN elevation of ALT or AST may be unnecessary as transient fluctuations are common and the liver can adapt

rules.rule_text: All trial subjects showing possible DILI must be followed until all abnormalities return to normal or to the baseline state. DILI may develop or progress even after the causative drug has been stopped. Results must be recorded on the case report form and in the database.
rules.source_section: IV.A.7. Follow-Up to Resolution
rules.verbatim_quote: All trial subjects showing possible DILI should be followed until all abnormalities return to normal or to the baseline state. DILI may develop or progress even after the causative drug has been stopped. Results should be recorded on the case report form and in the database.

rules.rule_text: Rechallenge of subjects with significant AT elevations (>5xULN) generally should not be attempted. If rechallenge is considered, it requires: important benefit to the subject from the drug; absence of other treatment options or accumulated data showing no severe DILI potential; subject informed consent; and institutional review board consultation.
rules.source_section: IV.A.8. Rechallenge
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, rechallenge of subjects with significant AT elevations (>5xULN) should not be attempted... Rechallenge can be considered if the subject has shown important benefit from the drug and other options are not available or if substantial accumulated data with the test drug do not show a potential for severe injury. The subject should be made aware of the potential risk, and consent to the rechallenge, and the institutional review board consulted.

rules.rule_text: A rechallenge may not be considered negative unless the subject is exposed to and tolerates the same dose and treatment duration that preceded the original reaction. A negative rechallenge does not necessarily allow a conclusion that the drug did not cause the original injury, as most people can adapt to xenobiotic substances.
rules.source_section: IV.A.8. Rechallenge
rules.verbatim_quote: Rechallenge may not be considered negative unless the subject is exposed to and tolerates the same dose and treatment duration that preceded the original reaction. A negative rechallenge does not necessarily allow a conclusion that the drug did not cause the injury.

rules.rule_text: Every clinical trial must include case report form (CRF) pages specifically designed to capture information pertinent to treatment-emergent liver abnormalities. CRFs for liver injury cases must include: time/date from drug start to illness; cessation/interruption of drug; complete description of injury including systemic symptoms; outcomes (death, transplant, hospitalization); time course of liver enzyme/bilirubin abnormalities; all concomitant drugs; and evaluation of nondrug causes.
rules.source_section: IV.C. Case Report Forms
rules.verbatim_quote: Every clinical trial should include case report form pages specifically designed to capture information pertinent to the evaluation of treatment-emergent liver abnormalities... case report forms and narratives should include the following information for cases in which liver injury is found (including control subjects with such injury): Time and date from start of drug administration to start of illness. Time and date of cessation of drug, or interruption of drug administration. Complete description of the injury, including systemic symptoms, other organ involvement, rash, fever, and eosinophilia. Outcomes such as death, liver transplant, hospitalization, recovery, and treatment for DILI.

rules.rule_text: Any potential Hy's Law case must be handled as a serious unexpected adverse event associated with the use of the drug and reported to FDA promptly (i.e., even before all other possible causes of liver injury have been excluded). It must be reported before fully working up the patient to rule out other etiologies.
rules.source_section: IV.C. Case Report Forms
rules.verbatim_quote: Any potential Hy's Law case should be handled as a serious unexpected adverse event associated with the use of the drug and reported to the FDA promptly (i.e., even before all other possible causes of liver injury have been excluded). It should be promptly reported to the FDA before fully working up the patient to rule out other etiologies.

rules.rule_text: Slight AT abnormalities (to <3xULN) are common in untreated and placebo-treated subjects and are not informative about the potential for severe DILI. Standard practice is to focus on AT values ≥3x-, 5x-, or 10xULN and compare their incidence in drug-exposed subjects versus control groups.
rules.source_section: IV.D.1. Frequency and Magnitude of Liver AT Abnormalities
rules.verbatim_quote: Slight abnormalities of this kind (to <3xULN) are common in untreated and placebo-treated subjects and are not informative about the potential for the development of severe DILI. Subjects with such abnormalities should be watched. Therefore, it has become standard practice to look at greater deviations, such as AT values ≥3x-, 5x-, or 10xULN.

rules.rule_text: When AT abnormalities indicating hepatocellular injury are accompanied by evidence of impaired hepatic function (bilirubin elevation >2xULN) in the absence of biliary obstruction or other explanation, the combined finding (Hy's Law case) represents a signal of drug potential to cause severe DILI. Even one or two well-documented cases of this combination is ominous.
rules.source_section: IV.D.2. Combined Elevations of Aminotransferases and Bilirubin
rules.verbatim_quote: When AT abnormalities indicating hepatocellular injury are accompanied by evidence of impaired hepatic function (bilirubin elevation >2xULN), in the absence of evidence of biliary obstruction... the combined finding (i.e., Hy's Law cases) represents a signal of a potential for the drug to cause severe DILI. Experience has indicated that the occurrence of even one or two well-documented cases of this combination is ominous, indicating a likelihood that the drug will cause severe liver injury.

rules.rule_text: NDA and BLA submissions must include an assessment of drug metabolism, specifying whether the drug is metabolized to hepatotoxic metabolites and whether it is oxidatively metabolized by the CYP450 system. The metabolism of a drug can markedly affect its hepatotoxic safety profile.
rules.source_section: IV.E.1. Assessment of Drug Metabolism
rules.verbatim_quote: The metabolism of a drug can markedly affect the safety profile of the drug. A drug may be metabolized to a hepatotoxic metabolite (e.g., acetaminophen, halothane, isoniazid). Most hepatotoxic drugs have been oxidatively metabolized by the CYP450 system.

rules.rule_text: Applicants must provide an analysis of the incidence of AT, bilirubin, and ALP abnormalities seen in subjects in controlled trials with at least one dose of drug exposure. Incidences for pooled data must include: 3x-, 5x-, 10x-, and 20xULN elevations of AST, ALT; TBL >2xULN; ALP >1.5xULN; AT >3xULN with elevated bilirubin; AT elevations in temporal association with hepatic symptoms; and liver-related deaths and discontinuations.
rules.source_section: IV.E.2. Assessment of Liver-Related Adverse Events in Controlled Trials
rules.verbatim_quote: Applicants should provide an analysis of the incidence of abnormalities in AT, bilirubin, and ALP levels seen in subjects in controlled trials with at least one dose of drug exposure... Incidences for pooled data should include, but are not limited to: 3x-, 5x-, 10x-, and 20xULN elevations of AST, ALT, and either ALT or AST. Any elevations of bilirubin; elevated TBL to >2xULN. Any elevations of ALP >1.5xULN. Elevation of AT (>3xULN) accompanied by elevated bilirubin (>1.5xULN, >2xULN). Elevation of AT in temporal association with nausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal pain, or fatigue. Possibly liver-related deaths and liver-related treatment discontinuations.

rules.rule_text: All incidences of liver-related adverse events must be calculated separately for drug-, placebo-, and active-controlled groups. Normal ranges for all tests must be provided. Time-to-event analyses for events occurring with increased incidence must be provided. The contribution of sex, age, risk factors, and drug dose or regimen to the abnormalities must be explored.
rules.source_section: IV.E.2. Assessment of Liver-Related Adverse Events in Controlled Trials
rules.verbatim_quote: All incidences should be calculated separately for drug-, placebo-, and active-controlled groups. Normal ranges for all tests should be provided. Time-to-event analyses for events occurring with increased incidence should be provided (e.g., elevated AT, bilirubin). The contribution of sex, age, risk factors, and drug dose or regimen to the abnormalities seen should be explored.

rules.rule_text: Applicants must provide liver-related adverse event analysis for the entire clinical trials database, including subjects with at least one dose of trial drug in phase 1 or phase 2 trials or in uncontrolled, open-label, extension trials. This analysis must include time-to-event analyses of events occurring at increased incidence, and rates of death and trial withdrawal.
rules.source_section: IV.E.3. Assessment of Liver-Related Adverse Events in the Entire Clinical Trials Database
rules.verbatim_quote: Applicants should provide an analysis of the incidence of abnormalities in AT, bilirubin, and ALP levels for the entire clinical trials database, including subjects with exposure of at least one dose of trial drug in phase 1 or phase 2 trials, or in uncontrolled, open label, extension trials.

rules.rule_text: NDA and BLA submissions must include a listing of possible Hy's Law cases identified by treatment group (subjects with elevated AT >3xULN, ALP <2xULN, and bilirubin ≥2xULN). A narrative summary for each Hy's Law case must be provided, synthesizing all available clinical data including subject demographics, signs/symptoms, exposure-dose relationship, concomitant drugs, test results, dechallenge/rechallenge results, and outcomes.
rules.source_section: IV.E.4. Assessment of Hy's Law Cases in the Clinical Trials Database
rules.verbatim_quote: NDA and BLA submissions should include a listing of possible Hy's Law cases identified by treatment group (e.g., subjects with any elevated AT of >3xULN, ALP <2xULN, and associated with an increase in bilirubin ≥2xULN). A narrative summary for each Hy's Law case should be provided.

rules.rule_text: When a drug has been marketed in other countries, the applicant must provide a synopsis of the global safety experience and level of usage, and must describe in detail all cases of hepatotoxicity observed or suspected in the foreign marketing experience.
rules.source_section: IV.E.4. Assessment of Hy's Law Cases
rules.verbatim_quote: In some cases, a drug under consideration in the United States will have been marketed in other countries. In these cases it is important for the applicant to provide a synopsis of the global safety experience and level of usage and to describe in detail all cases of hepatotoxicity observed or suspected.

rules.rule_text: The overall DILI assessment in the NDA or BLA must characterize a drug's potential for DILI by addressing: (1) adequacy of liver monitoring; (2) whether there were cases of probable severe DILI; (3) how DILI signals (AT elevations, Hy's Law cases) were assessed; (4) which doses and durations of exposure were associated with hepatotoxicity signals; (5) expected postmarketing incidence of mild, moderate, and severe DILI; (6) whether trial information is sufficient for overall benefit-risk assessment; and (7) whether there was sufficient drug exposure to set an upper boundary for risk of severe DILI.
rules.source_section: IV.E.5. Overall Assessment of a Drug's Potential to Cause DILI
rules.verbatim_quote: The overall assessment should characterize a drug's potential for DILI and should consider at least the following questions: Was liver monitoring sufficiently frequent and thorough to characterize DILI risk? Were there any cases of probable severe DILI? Were there signals of a potential for DILI (e.g., AT elevations, Hy's Law cases) and how were these signals assessed? What doses and durations of exposure were associated with hepatotoxicity signals? What approximate incidence of mild, moderate, and severe DILI can be expected postmarketing? Is the trial information sufficient to inform an overall risk-benefit assessment? Was there sufficient drug exposure (i.e., number of trial subjects and duration of treatment of each trial subject) and adequate liver test monitoring to reliably set an upper boundary for risk of severe DILI after marketing?

rules.rule_text: Alternative causes of liver test abnormalities must be systematically evaluated during close observation. Acute viral hepatitis (A, B, C, D, E), alcoholic and autoimmune hepatitis, hepatobiliary disorders (gallstones, biliary tract disease), NASH, cardiovascular causes (ischemic hepatitis from right heart failure/hypotension), and concomitant hepatotoxic medications must all be considered and documented.
rules.source_section: IV.A.6. Evaluating Data for Alternative Causes
rules.verbatim_quote: An important purpose of close observation is to gather additional clinical information to seek other possible causes of the observed liver test abnormalities, such as one of the following common causes: Acute viral hepatitis... Alcoholic and autoimmune hepatitis... Hepatobiliary disorders... NASH... Cardiovascular causes... Concomitant treatments.

rules.rule_text: It is critical to discover concomitant treatments, including exposure to nonprescription and dietary supplement products that might be responsible for liver injury. Subjects must be specifically questioned about herbal/dietary supplements, acetaminophen, and occupational chemical agents, as these may not be volunteered unless specifically asked.
rules.source_section: IV.A.6. Evaluating Data for Alternative Causes — Concomitant Treatments
rules.verbatim_quote: It is critical to discover concomitant treatments, including exposure to nonprescription and dietary supplement products that might be responsible for injury... The possible exposure to potentially toxic herbal or dietary supplement mixtures (sometimes of unknown composition), nonprescription medications such as acetaminophen, or to occupational chemical agents may not be volunteered unless subjects are specifically questioned.

rules.rule_text: The availability of liver biopsy, explant, or autopsy slides for pathology review is valuable in FDA's assessment of Hy's Law cases. Reports of external consultant opinions solicited by the applicant must be provided to FDA.
rules.source_section: IV.E.4. Assessment of Hy's Law Cases
rules.verbatim_quote: The availability of liver biopsy, explant, or autopsy slides for pathology review by review staff or external expert consultants has been helpful in the FDA's assessment of Hy's Law cases. Reports of external consultant opinions solicited by the applicant should be provided to the FDA.

rules.rule_text: Complete narrative summaries must be provided for all subjects who died of hepatic illness or who discontinued trial drugs for hepatotoxicity, including subjects with abnormalities consistent with protocol-specific stopping rules.
rules.source_section: IV.E.4. Assessment of Hy's Law Cases
rules.verbatim_quote: Applicants also should provide complete narrative summaries that include the components previously listed for all subjects who died of hepatic illness, or who discontinued trial drugs for hepatotoxicity, including subjects with abnormalities consistent with protocol-specific stopping rules.

rules.rule_text: Normalization of AT abnormalities on continued treatment is not proof that the abnormality was not drug-caused, as it can result from liver adaptation to the drug. Continuing drug administration is unacceptably dangerous if there is marked serum aminotransferase elevation or evidence of functional impairment (rising bilirubin or INR).
rules.source_section: IV.D.1. Frequency and Magnitude — Interpretation
rules.verbatim_quote: normalization of abnormalities on continued treatment is not proof that the abnormality was not drug-caused, as it can result from liver adaptation to the drug... continuing drug appears unacceptably dangerous if there is marked serum aminotransferase elevation or evidence of functional impairment, as indicated by rising bilirubin or INR, which represent substantial liver injury.

rules.rule_text: A finding of hepatocellular injury (elevated serum ATs) is a necessary but not sufficient signal of potential to cause severe DILI. It is only those drugs that can cause hepatocellular injury extensive enough to reduce the liver's functional ability to clear bilirubin or synthesize prothrombin and other coagulation factors that cause severe DILI.
rules.source_section: II. Background: DILI — Hepatocellular Injury
rules.verbatim_quote: Evidence of hepatocellular injury is thus a necessary, but not sufficient, signal of the potential to cause severe DILI... It is only those drugs that can cause hepatocellular injury extensive enough to reduce the liver's functional ability to clear bilirubin from the plasma or to synthesize prothrombin and other coagulation factors that cause severe DILI.

rules.rule_text: The most severe DILI reactions are idiosyncratic — not predictable or clearly dose-related, occurring at doses well-tolerated by most patients and dependent on individual susceptibility. These reactions generally have rates of death or transplantation of ≤1 per 10,000, so a single case rarely would be found even if several thousand subjects were studied.
rules.source_section: II. Background: DILI — Idiosyncratic DILI
rules.verbatim_quote: More difficult to detect is toxicity that is not predictable or clearly dose-related that occurs at doses well tolerated by most people, but seems to depend on individual susceptibilities that have not as yet been characterized. Most of the drugs withdrawn from the market for hepatotoxicity have caused death or transplantation at frequencies in the range of ≤1 per 10,000, so that a single case of such an event rarely would be found even if several thousand subjects were studied.

rules.rule_text: The Rule of 3 applies to DILI risk estimation: to have a 95% probability of observing at least one Hy's Law case in a treated population, approximately 3,000 exposed subjects are needed (if the true incidence of severe DILI is 1/1,000). Absence of Hy's Law cases in 3,000 subjects sets the upper 95% confidence bound on severe DILI rate at ≤1/10,000.
rules.source_section: III. Signals of DILI and Hy's Law — Rule of 3
rules.verbatim_quote: if the true incidence of severe injury is 1/1,000, about 3,000 exposed subjects (Rule of 3) would be needed to have a 95 percent probability of observing at least one Hy's Law case in the treated population (Rosner 1995)... This calculation would then suggest a rate of expected severe liver injury ≤1 per 10,000 exposed patients.

rules.rule_text: Marked elevations of AT to 5x-, 10x-, or 20xULN in modest numbers of subjects in the test drug group and not seen (or seen much less frequently) in the control group indicate high sensitivity for predicting severe DILI, though specificity is suboptimal as some non-severely hepatotoxic drugs can cause such elevations.
rules.source_section: III. Signals of DILI and Hy's Law — High AT Elevations
rules.verbatim_quote: Marked elevations of AT to 5x-, 10x-, or 20xULN in modest numbers of subjects in the test drug group and not seen (or seen much less frequently) in the control group... Many, but not all, severely hepatotoxic drugs show such elevations, indicating high sensitivity for predicting severe DILI; again, however, some drugs, such as tacrine and others that are not severely hepatotoxic, also can cause AT elevations to this degree, so that specificity of this finding is suboptimal.

rules.rule_text: Increased plasma prothrombin time (PT), or its international normalized ratio (INR), as a consequence of reduced hepatic production of Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors, is a potentially useful measure of liver function that may suggest the potential for severe liver injury when accompanying hepatocellular injury findings.
rules.source_section: III. Signals of DILI and Hy's Law — INR as Liver Function Marker
rules.verbatim_quote: Increased plasma prothrombin time, or its international normalized ratio (INR), a consequence of reduced hepatic production of Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors, is another potentially useful measure of liver function that might suggest the potential for severe liver injury.
